title: Listening - Part 3: Private Space Exploration
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two aerospace experts, Thomas and Ada, discussing the rise of private space tourism. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the ethics and logistical challenges of commercial space travel.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Good morning. Today we are looking beyond our atmosphere. With the recent surge in private orbital flights, I’m joined by Thomas, a former mission commander, and Ada, a space policy analyst. Thomas, we’ve seen the images of billionaires in zero-G. Is this the democratic future of space we were promised, or just a playground for the elite?

Thomas: [15] It’s a bit of both, though I suspect the 'playground' label is a bit reductive. Look, every major leap in transportation technology—from the first transatlantic flights to the early days of the automobile—started as an expensive hobby for the wealthy. It’s the capital from these early adopters that funds the research and development required to make the technology robust and, eventually, affordable. [/15] What people forget is that these private missions are testing vital systems that NASA or the ESA simply don’t have the budget to experiment with as aggressively. So, while it looks like a joyride, it’s actually a high-stakes laboratory. 

Interviewer: Ada, do you agree? Or is the environmental cost too high for what is essentially a luxury good?

Ada: That’s the elephant in the room, isn't it? [16] We are in a climate crisis, and launching a rocket consumes an eye-watering amount of propellant and releases black carbon directly into the upper atmosphere. My concern isn't just the 'elitism,' but the total lack of international regulation regarding the carbon footprint of these flights. [/16] Currently, private companies have a fairly free hand, and if the frequency of launches increases ten-fold, as predicted, we could be looking at a significant new driver of ozone depletion. We need a global treaty before the genie is truly out of the bottle.

Interviewer: Thomas, you’ve spent months on the ISS. Does the physical reality of space travel match the glossy marketing we see today?

Thomas: [17] The marketing definitely glosses over the 'unglamorous' bits. They show you the view of the Earth, which is admittedly life-changing, but they don't show you the persistent nausea of space sickness or the psychological strain of being in a pressurized tin can where you can’t even open a window for a breeze. [/17] Space is hostile. It’s not a hotel stay; it’s a survival exercise. I think some of these 'tourists' are going to be in for a rude awakening when they realize they can't just 'check out' if they feel claustrophobic or bored.

Interviewer: Ada, what about the 'Orbital Debris' problem? Is that being addressed?

Ada: [18] It’s being talked about, but not solved. Every launch adds to the cloud of junk circling the planet. We are reaching a point, known as the Kessler Syndrome, where one collision could trigger a chain reaction that renders certain orbits unusable for generations. [/18] Private companies are focused on getting things *up* there, but there is very little financial incentive for them to bring things *down*. Without a 'space salvage' law, we are effectively turning our backyard into a minefield.

Interviewer: Is there still a role for national agencies like NASA?

Thomas: Absolutely. [19] Private companies are great at the 'taxi service'—getting to and from Low Earth Orbit. But they aren't interested in pure science that doesn't have a quick return on investment. If we want to understand the origins of the universe or the geology of Mars, that still requires the patient, taxpayer-funded deep-space exploration that only national governments can sustain. [/19] They are the pioneers; the private sector is the infrastructure.

Interviewer: A final thought for those dreaming of a holiday on the Moon?

Ada: [20] Don't pack your bags just yet. Beyond the technical hurdles, we have to settle the legalities. Who owns the Moon? Who has the right to mine it? We are entering a 'Wild West' phase of space, and history tells us that without clear rules, it usually ends in conflict rather than cooperation. [/20]

Interviewer: Thomas, Ada, thank you both for joining us.

questions.question: What point does Thomas make about the cost of private space flights?
options: A) It is likely to remain high for the foreseeable future.
B) It is a necessary stage in the evolution of the technology.
C) It is an unfair way to fund scientific research.
D) It reflects the true value of the resources consumed.
questions.explanation: Thomas compares it to cars and planes, saying early high costs fund the R&D needed to make it robust and affordable.

questions.question: Ada's main concern about the increase in space launches is
options: A) the potential for elite tourism to distract from science.
B) the lack of safety standards for civilian passengers.
C) the absence of international laws on environmental impact.
D) the waste of money that could be spent on Earth.
questions.explanation: She mentions the lack of regulation regarding the carbon footprint and the need for a global treaty.

questions.question: What is Thomas's opinion of the current space tourism marketing?
options: A) It accurately portrays the awe-inspiring views.
B) It focuses too much on the technical side of the mission.
C) It fails to mention the physical and mental hardships.
D) It creates unrealistic expectations of luxury.
questions.explanation: He says it glosses over 'unglamorous' bits like nausea and psychological strain.

questions.question: According to Ada, why is orbital debris such a significant threat?
options: A) It makes it too expensive to launch new satellites.
B) It could cause a catastrophic chain of collisions.
C) It is impossible to track accurately from Earth.
D) It interferes with deep-space scientific observations.
questions.explanation: She refers to the Kessler Syndrome, where one collision triggers a reaction that makes orbits unusable.

questions.question: Thomas argues that national space agencies are still needed to
options: A) regulate the activities of private companies.
B) train the next generation of commercial pilots.
C) conduct research with no immediate financial benefit.
D) manage the cleanup of orbital junk.
questions.explanation: He notes that private firms won't do pure science that doesn't have a quick return on investment.

questions.question: Ada concludes that the 'Wild West' nature of space travel
options: A) will encourage faster innovation in the sector.
B) is a natural part of any new frontier.
C) could lead to international disputes over resources.
D) will be solved once the technical hurdles are cleared.
questions.explanation: She warns that without clear rules, the situation usually ends in conflict.
